summary: Drew DeVault recaps a busy month of travel across Japan, Hawaii, and Seattle while maintaining steady progress on multiple free software projects. The Wayland book passed the halfway mark and sway 1.2 was released, with its light feature set signaling maturity. He highlights progress on aerc email client, mrsh shell, and a new ctools project aiming for full POSIX compliance. These projects collectively work toward his vision of a simple, elegant operating system, tracked at arewesimpleyet.org. SourceHut crossed 10,000 users and gained new features including anonymous ticket creation via email.
key_points: Sway 1.2's light feature set is presented as a positive sign of maturity rather than stagnation, showing the project is stabilizing
The ctools project aims to replace coreutils/busybox with minimal, single-file C implementations that have full POSIX.1-2017 conformance tests and man pages
Multiple projects (mrsh, ctools, cproc) are converging toward a vision of a simple, elegant operating system built from scratch
SourceHut reached 10,000 users and added the ability for non-account holders to interact with tickets via email, reinforcing the email-first workflow philosophy
money_quotes: I like this release because it's light on new features - showing that sway is maturing into a stable and reliable Wayland desktop.
Every command has full POSIX.1-2017 support with a conformance test and a man page, in one C source file and no dependencies.
We're starting to see a simple and elegant operating system come together - exactly the kind I wish we already had.
A day may soon come when computers become the again elegant and simple tools they were always meant to be!
At least if we assume "within a few decades" as a valid definition of "soon".
For me a "day off" is a day where I write less than 1,000 lines of code.
key_insight: A constellation of small, focused free software projects — a shell, coreutils, a compiler, a compositor — are quietly converging toward the simple, elegant operating system DeVault believes computers were always meant to run.
